agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A logistics platform introducing automated route planning for delivery drivers, combining real-time route optimization with vehicle constraint enforcement and proactive customer notifications. This sits at the intersection of fleet operations, last-mile delivery, and customer experience.","bounded_context":"This feature spans at least four bounded contexts: (1) Route Optimization — owns route calculation, recalculation triggers, and stop sequencing; (2) Fleet Management — owns driver shifts, vehicle classifications, and zone assignments; (3) Delivery Management — owns delivery lifecycle, pending/failed states, and zone association; (4) Customer Notification — owns notification triggers and the 'next 3 stops' delivery window concept. The spec conflates these, which risks coupling domains that should evolve independently.","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Shift","definition":"A bounded work period during which a driver is active and eligible for delivery assignments. A shift start is the trigger for initial route assignment."},{"term":"Optimized Route","definition":"An ordered sequence of delivery stops calculated by the system to minimize some objective function (unspecified — see escalations). Assigned to a driver for the duration of their shift or until recalculation."},{"term":"Zone","definition":"A geographic area that scopes both drivers and deliveries. A driver operates within a zone; deliveries belong to a zone. Zone boundaries and assignment rules are undefined in the spec."},{"term":"Recalculation Trigger","definition":"An event that invalidates the current optimized route and forces re-computation. Three defined triggers: new delivery added to zone, delivery marked as failed, driver manual deviation."},{"term":"Manual Deviation","definition":"When a driver departs from the system-suggested route sequence or path. Detection mechanism is unspecified."},{"term":"Next 3 Stops Window","definition":"A rolling notification boundary — when a delivery is among the next 3 upcoming stops on the driver's active route, the associated customer is notified."},{"term":"Licensed Vehicle Class","definition":"A classification of the driver's vehicle that constrains which deliveries can be assigned. Acts as a hard filter during route assignment."},{"term":"Estimated Stop Time","definition":"The default time budget per delivery stop (4 minutes), used in route time calculations. Overridable per zone by the operations team."},{"term":"Failed Delivery","definition":"A delivery that could not be completed at the stop. Triggers route recalculation to remove it from the active sequence."},{"term":"Pending Delivery","definition":"A delivery awaiting assignment and completion within a zone. The pool from which optimized routes are constructed."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"When a driver starts their shift, the system MUST automatically assign them an optimized route composed of all pending deliveries in their zone.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is an operational automation policy. The business could decide to allow manual route creation or batch assignment instead. The optimization strategy itself is a policy choice.","testable_assertion":"Given a driver starting a shift in zone Z with N pending deliveries, the system produces an ordered route containing exactly those N deliveries that are compatible with the driver's vehicle class."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"The route MUST be recalculated when a new delivery is added to the driver's active zone.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Recalculation policy could be changed to batch updates or threshold-based triggers without corrupting system state.","testable_assertion":"Given a driver with an active route in zone Z, when a new pending delivery is created in zone Z, then the driver's route is recalculated to include the new delivery."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"The route MUST be recalculated when a delivery on the route is marked as failed.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is operational policy — the business could instead choose to simply skip the stop without full recalculation.","testable_assertion":"Given a driver with an active route containing delivery D, when D is marked as failed, then the route is recalculated excluding D."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"The route MUST be recalculated when the driver manually deviates from the suggested route.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Deviation handling is a policy choice. The system could alternatively flag the deviation without recalculating.","testable_assertion":"Given a driver with an active route, when the system detects a manual deviation, then the route is recalculated from the driver's current position."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"A driver MUST NOT be assigned deliveries that exceed their licensed vehicle class capacity or type restrictions.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a physical and legal constraint. A motorcycle physically cannot carry freight, and assigning such a delivery violates licensing regulations. Violation means unsafe and illegal operations — system state is corrupt if this is breached.","testable_assertion":"For every delivery on a driver's route, delivery.requiredVehicleClass must be compatible with driver.licensedVehicleClass. No route — initial or recalculated — may ever contain an incompatible delivery."},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"Customers MUST be notified when their delivery enters the 'next 3 stops' window on the driver's active route.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The notification window size (3) is a business policy that could be adjusted. Notification itself is a customer experience choice, not a data integrity constraint.","testable_assertion":"When a route recalculation or stop completion causes delivery D to be within the next 3 stops, and the customer for D has not yet been notified for this route, then a notification is sent to the customer."},{"id":"BR-7","rule":"The default estimated time per delivery stop is 4 minutes, overridable per zone by the operations team.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The 4-minute default and override capability are purely operational parameters that can be changed by policy.","testable_assertion":"Route time estimates use 4 minutes per stop unless an operations override exists for the zone, in which case the override value is used."},{"id":"BR-8","rule":"A driver can only be assigned deliveries within their designated zone.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Zone boundaries enforce operational jurisdiction and potentially contractual/insurance coverage. Assigning cross-zone deliveries breaks the fundamental dispatching model and could violate labor agreements or insurance terms.","testable_assertion":"Every delivery on a driver's route must have delivery.zone == driver.activeZone."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A driver starts their shift in zone Z with 5 pending deliveries compatible with their vehicle class","when":"The shift is activated","then":"The system assigns an optimized route containing all 5 deliveries in an optimized sequence and the driver sees the route immediately"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A driver has an active route with 4 remaining stops in zone Z","when":"A new compatible delivery is added to zone Z","then":"The route is recalculated to include the new delivery, resulting in 5 stops in an optimized sequence"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A driver has an active route and delivery D is the 3rd stop","when":"Delivery D is marked as failed","then":"The route is recalculated excluding D, and remaining stops are re-optimized from the driver's current position"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"A driver has an active route and deviates from the prescribed path or sequence","when":"The system detects the deviation","then":"The route is recalculated from the driver's current position using remaining undelivered stops"},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A driver completes a stop and delivery D is now within the next 3 stops","when":"The route updates the driver's position","then":"The customer associated with delivery D receives a notification that their delivery is approaching"},{"id":"AC-6","given":"A motorcycle-class driver starts a shift in a zone containing both parcel and freight deliveries","when":"The route is calculated","then":"Only parcel deliveries compatible with motorcycle class are included; freight deliveries are excluded from the route"},{"id":"AC-7","given":"The operations team has set a 6-minute stop estimate override for zone Z","when":"A route is calculated for a driver in zone Z","then":"Each stop in the route uses 6 minutes for time estimation instead of the default 4 minutes"},{"id":"AC-8","given":"A driver has an active route and a customer's delivery is currently the 4th stop","when":"The 1st stop is completed, making that customer's delivery now the 3rd stop","then":"The customer is notified that their delivery is approaching"},{"id":"AC-9","given":"A driver starts their shift in a zone with no pending deliveries","when":"The shift is activated","then":"The driver is assigned an empty route and waits; when a delivery arrives in the zone, recalculation assigns it"},{"id":"AC-10","given":"Multiple recalculation triggers fire simultaneously (e.g., new delivery added and another marked failed)","when":"Both events are processed","then":"A single recalculation incorporates both changes rather than triggering two sequential recalculations"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Driver starts shift in zone with zero pending deliveries","expected_behavior":"Driver receives an empty route. When the first delivery appears in the zone, recalculation triggers and assigns it."},{"scenario":"All deliveries in a zone require a vehicle class no active driver possesses","expected_behavior":"Deliveries remain unassigned in the pending pool. The system should surface this as an operational alert — orphaned deliveries with no eligible driver."},{"scenario":"Recalculation cascade — recalculation triggers during an in-progress recalculation","expected_behavior":"The system must either queue the trigger and recalculate again after the current one completes, or debounce triggers and batch them into a single recalculation."},{"scenario":"Customer receives 'next 3 stops' notification but route is recalculated and their delivery is no longer in the window","expected_behavior":"Spec does not address this. Business must decide: retract/update notification, or accept that early notification may become inaccurate."},{"scenario":"Driver deviates to the location of a later stop (skip-ahead)","expected_behavior":"Deviation detection triggers recalculation. If the driver is at a valid stop, the system should recognize the stop and recalculate from there, not force the original sequence."},{"scenario":"Operations team changes zone stop time override while drivers have active routes","expected_behavior":"Unclear whether existing routes recalculate with new estimate or only new routes use the updated value. Business must decide."},{"scenario":"A delivery is reassigned from one zone to another while a driver in the original zone has it on their route","expected_behavior":"Should trigger recalculation for the original driver (delivery removed) and potentially for a driver in the new zone (delivery added)."},{"scenario":"Driver's shift ends while deliveries remain on their route","expected_behavior":"Undelivered stops must return to the pending pool for reassignment. Customers in the 'next 3' window may need updated notifications."},{"scenario":"Two drivers active in the same zone simultaneously","expected_behavior":"The spec says 'all pending deliveries' are assigned. This creates a conflict — the system needs a partitioning strategy to split deliveries between drivers, not assign all to each."},{"scenario":"Driver completes deliveries faster or slower than the estimated stop time","expected_behavior":"Route time estimates drift from reality. The system should update ETAs based on actual progress, not just the static estimate."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Route optimization directly reduces fuel costs, increases deliveries per shift, and reduces overtime. Vehicle class enforcement prevents costly failed delivery attempts and potential liability from illegal vehicle use. Accurate ETAs improve first-attempt delivery success rates.","operational":"Automatic route assignment eliminates manual dispatching overhead. Real-time recalculation keeps routes responsive but introduces computational cost and complexity. The 4-minute default with zone overrides allows ops teams to fine-tune without engineering changes.","user":"Customers gain transparency via the 'next 3 stops' notification, reducing 'where is my delivery' support calls. Drivers get clear, optimized guidance rather than self-planning. However, frequent recalculations may frustrate drivers if routes change constantly mid-shift.","risk":"High dependency on real-time location data for deviation detection. Optimization algorithm choice significantly impacts route quality and computation time. Notification timing errors could erode customer trust. Vehicle class enforcement is safety-critical — any bypass is a liability event.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Drivers","impact":"Shifts from self-directed routing to system-directed. Reduces cognitive load but removes autonomy. Frequent recalculations may cause frustration and distrust of the system."},{"group":"Customers","impact":"Gain proactive delivery notifications. Expectation management improves but inaccurate notifications (due to recalculation) could damage trust."},{"group":"Operations Team","impact":"Gains per-zone configuration control over stop time estimates. Reduces manual dispatch workload. Needs monitoring tools for orphaned deliveries and zone coverage gaps."},{"group":"Fleet/Safety Compliance","impact":"Vehicle class enforcement provides automated compliance. Audit trail of assignments becomes critical for regulatory defense."},{"group":"Engineering/Platform","impact":"Must build or integrate a route optimization engine, real-time deviation detection, event-driven recalculation pipeline, and notification system. Significant architectural complexity."}]},"confidence":{"level":52,"reasoning":"The spec describes a coherent feature at a high level but leaves multiple operationally critical details ambiguous. The optimization objective is never stated, deviation detection has no defined mechanism, multi-driver zone partitioning is not addressed (and may contradict the 'all pending deliveries' language), and the notification lifecycle after recalculation is undefined. These are not contradictions — the spec is simply underspecified in ways that would block implementation.","high_confidence_areas":["Vehicle class constraint is clearly a hard invariant with legal/safety implications","The three recalculation triggers are explicitly enumerated","Customer notification at 'next 3 stops' is a clear threshold","Default 4-minute stop time with ops override is well-defined"],"low_confidence_areas":["What optimization objective is used — shortest distance, fastest time, priority-weighted, or multi-objective?","How deviation detection works — GPS geofencing, stop sequence violation, or manual driver input?","How deliveries are partitioned when multiple drivers are active in the same zone","Whether notification should be retracted or updated after recalculation moves a delivery out of the 3-stop window","Whether zone stop time overrides apply retroactively to active routes","What 'vehicle class compatibility' means concretely — is it a hierarchy, a tag system, or a matrix?"],"assumptions":["Each driver is assigned to exactly one zone per shift","Zone boundaries are pre-defined and do not overlap","Vehicle class compatibility is a simple type match (not a capacity calculation)","Route optimization runs synchronously and returns results fast enough for real-time use","Delivery failure is a terminal state for that delivery attempt (not retried on the same route)"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The optimization objective for route calculation is never specified. 'Optimized' could mean shortest distance, shortest time, highest priority first, maximum deliveries per hour, or a weighted combination. This fundamentally changes the algorithm, infrastructure requirements, and business outcomes. Different stakeholders likely have different expectations of what 'optimized' means.","affected_stakeholders":["Drivers","Operations Team","Engineering","Customers"],"options":["Optimize for shortest total travel time (most common in last-mile)","Optimize for shortest distance (fuel cost focus)","Optimize for delivery priority then time (SLA-driven)","Multi-objective optimization with configurable weights per zone"],"recommendation":"Default to shortest travel time as primary objective with delivery priority as a tiebreaker, since last-mile logistics almost universally optimizes for time. Make the objective configurable per zone by the operations team, consistent with the zone-level override pattern already established for stop time."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Manual deviation' has no defined detection mechanism. It could mean: the driver takes a different road (GPS path deviation), the driver skips a stop and goes to a later one (sequence deviation), or the driver drives away from all planned stops (abandonment). Each requires different detection logic and has different recalculation semantics.","affected_stakeholders":["Drivers","Engineering","Operations Team"],"options":["GPS geofence deviation — driver leaves corridor around planned path","Sequence deviation — driver arrives at a stop that is not the next expected stop","Explicit driver action — driver taps 'reroute' or 'skip' in the app","Combination: sequence deviation as primary, with GPS deviation as secondary"],"recommendation":"Define deviation as sequence deviation (driver completes or skips a stop out of order) as the primary trigger, since it is deterministic and does not require continuous GPS path matching. GPS path deviation is expensive to compute and prone to false positives from road detours."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The spec says the route includes 'all pending deliveries in their zone,' but does not address the scenario where multiple drivers are active in the same zone simultaneously. Assigning ALL pending deliveries to EACH driver creates duplicate assignments. The system needs a delivery partitioning or claiming strategy.","affected_stakeholders":["Drivers","Operations Team","Customers"],"options":["Partition deliveries geographically within the zone (sub-zones per driver)","Partition by optimization solver — assign to whichever driver yields the best overall fleet outcome","First-come assignment — each delivery assigned to the first driver whose route is calculated","Allow overlapping assignment with a claiming mechanism at pickup"],"recommendation":"Use optimization-based partitioning: when multiple drivers are in a zone, the route optimizer should consider all drivers simultaneously and partition deliveries to minimize total fleet travel time. This is the standard approach in vehicle routing problems (VRP) and avoids both duplication and suboptimal splits."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"No specification of what happens to customer notifications when recalculation moves a delivery out of the 'next 3 stops' window after a notification was already sent. The customer may have rearranged their schedule based on the notification.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Operations Team","Customer Support"],"options":["Send an updated notification with new ETA when delivery leaves the 3-stop window","Do not retract — accept that early notifications may become temporarily inaccurate","Send a 'delay' notification only if the delivery moves beyond a threshold (e.g., from stop 3 to stop 8+)"],"recommendation":"Send an updated notification if the delivery moves outside the 3-stop window after initial notification, with a simple message like 'Your delivery time has been updated.' Silence erodes trust more than an update does. Include threshold logic to avoid notification spam from minor reorderings."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}

This sits at the intersection of fleet operations, last-mile delivery, and customer experience.","bounded_context":"This feature spans at least four bounded contexts: (1) Route Optimization — owns route calculation, recalculation triggers, and stop sequencing; (2) Fl

content.thinking:
Let me analyze this logistics feature description carefully before producing my JSON output.

The feature is "Smart Route Optimization" for a logistics platform. Let me break down the bounded contexts, business rules, and acceptance criteria.

Bounded Contexts:
1. Route Optimization - route calculation, recalculation triggers, stop ordering
2. Fleet/Driver Management - driver shifts, vehicle classes, zone assignments
3. Delivery Management - pending deliveries, delivery status (failed, etc.)
4. Customer Notification - next 3 stops window notifications

Business Rules:
- Auto-assign optimized route on shift start


- Recalculate routes when new deliveries arrive in a zone, when deliveries fail, or when drivers manually deviate from the assigned path
- Notify customers within a 3-stop window before their delivery
- Enforce vehicle constraints like preventing motorcycles from carrying freight
- Use a default 4-minute stop duration that operations can override per zone

Now I'm identifying the key ambiguities that need clarification: zones could be defined geographically or administratively, "manual deviation" might mean GPS drift or intentional stop skipping, and the optimization criteria itself isn't specified—whether it prioritizes distance, time, delivery priority, or cost. I also need to understand what happens to the driver's view during route recalculation and whether they can reject changes.

Different stakeholders likely have different expectations of what 'optimized' means.","affected_stakeholders":["Drivers","Operations Team","Engineering","Customers"],"options":["Optimize for shortest total travel time (most common in last-mile)","Optimize for shortest distance (fuel cost focus)","Optimize for delivery priority then time (SLA-driven)","Multi-objective optimization with configurable weights per zone"],"recommendation":"Default to shortest travel time as primary objective with delivery priority as a tiebreaker, since last-mile logistics almost universally optimizes for time. Make the objective configurable per zone by the operations team, consistent with the zone-level override pattern already established for stop time."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Manual deviation' has no defined detection mechanism. It could mean: the driver takes a different road (GPS path deviation), the driver skips a stop and goes to a later one (sequence deviation), or the driver drives away from all planned stops (abandonment). Each requires different detection logic and has different recalculation semantics.","affected_stakeholders":["Drivers","Engineering","Operations Team"],"options":["GPS geofence deviation — driver leaves corridor around planned path","Sequence deviation — driver arrives at a stop that is not the next expected stop","Explicit driver action — driver taps 'reroute' or 'skip' in the app","Combination: sequence deviation as primary, with GPS deviation as secondary"],"recommendation":"Define deviation as sequence deviation (driver completes or skips a stop out of order) as the primary trigger, since it is deterministic and does not require continuous GPS path matching. GPS path deviation is expensive to compute and prone to false positives from road detours."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The spec says the route includes 'all pending deliveries in their zone,' but does not address the scenario where multiple drivers are active in the same zone simultaneously. Assigning ALL pending deliveries to EACH driver creates duplicate assignments. The system needs a delivery partitioning or claiming strategy.","affected_stakeholders":["Drivers","Operations Team","Customers"],"options":["Partition deliveries geographically within the zone (sub-zones per driver)","Partition by optimization solver — assign to whichever driver yields the best overall fleet outcome","First-come assignment — each delivery assigned to the first driver whose route is calculated","Allow overlapping assignment with a claiming mechanism at pickup"],"recommendation":"Use optimization-based partitioning: when multiple drivers are in a zone, the route optimizer should consider all drivers simultaneously and partition deliveries to minimize total fleet travel time. This is the standard approach in vehicle routing problems (VRP) and avoids both duplication and suboptimal splits."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"No specification of what happens to customer notifications when recalculation moves a delivery out of the 'next 3 stops' window after a notification was already sent. The customer may have rearranged their schedule based on the notification.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Operations Team","Customer Support"],"options":["Send an updated notification with new ETA when delivery leaves the 3-stop window","Do not retract — accept that early notifications may become temporarily inaccurate","Send a 'delay' notification only if the delivery moves beyond a threshold (e.g., from stop 3 to stop 8+)"],"recommendation":"Send an updated notification if the delivery moves outside the 3-stop window after initial notification, with a simple message like 'Your delivery time has been updated.' Silence erodes trust more than an update does. Include threshold logic to avoid notification spam from minor reorderings."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}

trace.result: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A logistics platform introducing automated route planning for delivery drivers, combining real-time route optimization with vehicle constraint enforcement and proactive customer notifications. This sits at the intersection of fleet operations, last-mile delivery, and customer experience.","bounded_context":"This feature spans at least four bounded contexts: (1) Route Optimization — owns route calculation, recalculation triggers, and stop sequencing; (2) Fleet Management — owns driver shifts, vehicle classifications, and zone assignments; (3) Delivery Management — owns delivery lifecycle, pending/failed states, and zone association; (4) Customer Notification — owns notification triggers and the 'next 3 stops' delivery window concept. The spec conflates these, which risks coupling domains that should evolve independently.","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Shift","definition":"A bounded work period during which a driver is active and eligible for delivery assignments. A shift start is the trigger for initial route assignment."},{"term":"Optimized Route","definition":"An ordered sequence of delivery stops calculated by the system to minimize some objective function (unspecified — see escalations). Assigned to a driver for the duration of their shift or until recalculation."},{"term":"Zone","definition":"A geographic area that scopes both drivers and deliveries. A driver operates within a zone; deliveries belong to a zone. Zone boundaries and assignment rules are undefined in the spec."},{"term":"Recalculation Trigger","definition":"An event that invalidates the current optimized route and forces re-computation. Three defined triggers: new delivery added to zone, delivery marked as failed, driver manual deviation."},{"term":"Manual Deviation","definition":"When a driver departs from the system-suggested route sequence or path. Detection mechanism is unspecified."},{"term":"Next 3 Stops Window","definition":"A rolling notification boundary — when a delivery is among the next 3 upcoming stops on the driver's active route, the associated customer is notified."},{"term":"Licensed Vehicle Class","definition":"A classification of the driver's vehicle that constrains which deliveries can be assigned. Acts as a hard filter during route assignment."},{"term":"Estimated Stop Time","definition":"The default time budget per delivery stop (4 minutes), used in route time calculations. Overridable per zone by the operations team."},{"term":"Failed Delivery","definition":"A delivery that could not be completed at the stop. Triggers route recalculation to remove it from the active sequence."},{"term":"Pending Delivery","definition":"A delivery awaiting assignment and completion within a zone. The pool from which optimized routes are constructed."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"When a driver starts their shift, the system MUST automatically assign them an optimized route composed of all pending deliveries in their zone.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is an operational automation policy. The business could decide to allow manual route creation or batch assignment instead. The optimization strategy itself is a policy choice.","testable_assertion":"Given a driver starting a shift in zone Z with N pending deliveries, the system produces an ordered route containing exactly those N deliveries that are compatible with the driver's vehicle class."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"The route MUST be recalculated when a new delivery is added to the driver's active zone.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Recalculation policy could be changed to batch updates or threshold-based triggers without corrupting system state.","testable_assertion":"Given a driver with an active route in zone Z, when a new pending delivery is created in zone Z, then the driver's route is recalculated to include the new delivery."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"The route MUST be recalculated when a delivery on the route is marked as failed.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is operational policy — the business could instead choose to simply skip the stop without full recalculation.","testable_assertion":"Given a driver with an active route containing delivery D, when D is marked as failed, then the route is recalculated excluding D."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"The route MUST be recalculated when the driver manually deviates from the suggested route.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Deviation handling is a policy choice. The system could alternatively flag the deviation without recalculating.","testable_assertion":"Given a driver with an active route, when the system detects a manual deviation, then the route is recalculated from the driver's current position."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"A driver MUST NOT be assigned deliveries that exceed their licensed vehicle class capacity or type restrictions.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a physical and legal constraint. A motorcycle physically cannot carry freight, and assigning such a delivery violates licensing regulations. Violation means unsafe and illegal operations — system state is corrupt if this is breached.","testable_assertion":"For every delivery on a driver's route, delivery.requiredVehicleClass must be compatible with driver.licensedVehicleClass. No route — initial or recalculated — may ever contain an incompatible delivery."},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"Customers MUST be notified when their delivery enters the 'next 3 stops' window on the driver's active route.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The notification window size (3) is a business policy that could be adjusted. Notification itself is a customer experience choice, not a data integrity constraint.","testable_assertion":"When a route recalculation or stop completion causes delivery D to be within the next 3 stops, and the customer for D has not yet been notified for this route, then a notification is sent to the customer."},{"id":"BR-7","rule":"The default estimated time per delivery stop is 4 minutes, overridable per zone by the operations team.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The 4-minute default and override capability are purely operational parameters that can be changed by policy.","testable_assertion":"Route time estimates use 4 minutes per stop unless an operations override exists for the zone, in which case the override value is used."},{"id":"BR-8","rule":"A driver can only be assigned deliveries within their designated zone.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Zone boundaries enforce operational jurisdiction and potentially contractual/insurance coverage. Assigning cross-zone deliveries breaks the fundamental dispatching model and could violate labor agreements or insurance terms.","testable_assertion":"Every delivery on a driver's route must have delivery.zone == driver.activeZone."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A driver starts their shift in zone Z with 5 pending deliveries compatible with their vehicle class","when":"The shift is activated","then":"The system assigns an optimized route containing all 5 deliveries in an optimized sequence and the driver sees the route immediately"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A driver has an active route with 4 remaining stops in zone Z","when":"A new compatible delivery is added to zone Z","then":"The route is recalculated to include the new delivery, resulting in 5 stops in an optimized sequence"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A driver has an active route and delivery D is the 3rd stop","when":"Delivery D is marked as failed","then":"The route is recalculated excluding D, and remaining stops are re-optimized from the driver's current position"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"A driver has an active route and deviates from the prescribed path or sequence","when":"The system detects the deviation","then":"The route is recalculated from the driver's current position using remaining undelivered stops"},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A driver completes a stop and delivery D is now within the next 3 stops","when":"The route updates the driver's position","then":"The customer associated with delivery D receives a notification that their delivery is approaching"},{"id":"AC-6","given":"A motorcycle-class driver starts a shift in a zone containing both parcel and freight deliveries","when":"The route is calculated","then":"Only parcel deliveries compatible with motorcycle class are included; freight deliveries are excluded from the route"},{"id":"AC-7","given":"The operations team has set a 6-minute stop estimate override for zone Z","when":"A route is calculated for a driver in zone Z","then":"Each stop in the route uses 6 minutes for time estimation instead of the default 4 minutes"},{"id":"AC-8","given":"A driver has an active route and a customer's delivery is currently the 4th stop","when":"The 1st stop is completed, making that customer's delivery now the 3rd stop","then":"The customer is notified that their delivery is approaching"},{"id":"AC-9","given":"A driver starts their shift in a zone with no pending deliveries","when":"The shift is activated","then":"The driver is assigned an empty route and waits; when a delivery arrives in the zone, recalculation assigns it"},{"id":"AC-10","given":"Multiple recalculation triggers fire simultaneously (e.g., new delivery added and another marked failed)","when":"Both events are processed","then":"A single recalculation incorporates both changes rather than triggering two sequential recalculations"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Driver starts shift in zone with zero pending deliveries","expected_behavior":"Driver receives an empty route. When the first delivery appears in the zone, recalculation triggers and assigns it."},{"scenario":"All deliveries in a zone require a vehicle class no active driver possesses","expected_behavior":"Deliveries remain unassigned in the pending pool. The system should surface this as an operational alert — orphaned deliveries with no eligible driver."},{"scenario":"Recalculation cascade — recalculation triggers during an in-progress recalculation","expected_behavior":"The system must either queue the trigger and recalculate again after the current one completes, or debounce triggers and batch them into a single recalculation."},{"scenario":"Customer receives 'next 3 stops' notification but route is recalculated and their delivery is no longer in the window","expected_behavior":"Spec does not address this. Business must decide: retract/update notification, or accept that early notification may become inaccurate."},{"scenario":"Driver deviates to the location of a later stop (skip-ahead)","expected_behavior":"Deviation detection triggers recalculation. If the driver is at a valid stop, the system should recognize the stop and recalculate from there, not force the original sequence."},{"scenario":"Operations team changes zone stop time override while drivers have active routes","expected_behavior":"Unclear whether existing routes recalculate with new estimate or only new routes use the updated value. Business must decide."},{"scenario":"A delivery is reassigned from one zone to another while a driver in the original zone has it on their route","expected_behavior":"Should trigger recalculation for the original driver (delivery removed) and potentially for a driver in the new zone (delivery added)."},{"scenario":"Driver's shift ends while deliveries remain on their route","expected_behavior":"Undelivered stops must return to the pending pool for reassignment. Customers in the 'next 3' window may need updated notifications."},{"scenario":"Two drivers active in the same zone simultaneously","expected_behavior":"The spec says 'all pending deliveries' are assigned. This creates a conflict — the system needs a partitioning strategy to split deliveries between drivers, not assign all to each."},{"scenario":"Driver completes deliveries faster or slower than the estimated stop time","expected_behavior":"Route time estimates drift from reality. The system should update ETAs based on actual progress, not just the static estimate."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Route optimization directly reduces fuel costs, increases deliveries per shift, and reduces overtime. Vehicle class enforcement prevents costly failed delivery attempts and potential liability from illegal vehicle use. Accurate ETAs improve first-attempt delivery success rates.","operational":"Automatic route assignment eliminates manual dispatching overhead. Real-time recalculation keeps routes responsive but introduces computational cost and complexity. The 4-minute default with zone overrides allows ops teams to fine-tune without engineering changes.","user":"Customers gain transparency via the 'next 3 stops' notification, reducing 'where is my delivery' support calls. Drivers get clear, optimized guidance rather than self-planning. However, frequent recalculations may frustrate drivers if routes change constantly mid-shift.","risk":"High dependency on real-time location data for deviation detection. Optimization algorithm choice significantly impacts route quality and computation time. Notification timing errors could erode customer trust. Vehicle class enforcement is safety-critical — any bypass is a liability event.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Drivers","impact":"Shifts from self-directed routing to system-directed. Reduces cognitive load but removes autonomy. Frequent recalculations may cause frustration and distrust of the system."},{"group":"Customers","impact":"Gain proactive delivery notifications. Expectation management improves but inaccurate notifications (due to recalculation) could damage trust."},{"group":"Operations Team","impact":"Gains per-zone configuration control over stop time estimates. Reduces manual dispatch workload. Needs monitoring tools for orphaned deliveries and zone coverage gaps."},{"group":"Fleet/Safety Compliance","impact":"Vehicle class enforcement provides automated compliance. Audit trail of assignments becomes critical for regulatory defense."},{"group":"Engineering/Platform","impact":"Must build or integrate a route optimization engine, real-time deviation detection, event-driven recalculation pipeline, and notification system. Significant architectural complexity."}]},"confidence":{"level":52,"reasoning":"The spec describes a coherent feature at a high level but leaves multiple operationally critical details ambiguous. The optimization objective is never stated, deviation detection has no defined mechanism, multi-driver zone partitioning is not addressed (and may contradict the 'all pending deliveries' language), and the notification lifecycle after recalculation is undefined. These are not contradictions — the spec is simply underspecified in ways that would block implementation.","high_confidence_areas":["Vehicle class constraint is clearly a hard invariant with legal/safety implications","The three recalculation triggers are explicitly enumerated","Customer notification at 'next 3 stops' is a clear threshold","Default 4-minute stop time with ops override is well-defined"],"low_confidence_areas":["What optimization objective is used — shortest distance, fastest time, priority-weighted, or multi-objective?","How deviation detection works — GPS geofencing, stop sequence violation, or manual driver input?","How deliveries are partitioned when multiple drivers are active in the same zone","Whether notification should be retracted or updated after recalculation moves a delivery out of the 3-stop window","Whether zone stop time overrides apply retroactively to active routes","What 'vehicle class compatibility' means concretely — is it a hierarchy, a tag system, or a matrix?"],"assumptions":["Each driver is assigned to exactly one zone per shift","Zone boundaries are pre-defined and do not overlap","Vehicle class compatibility is a simple type match (not a capacity calculation)","Route optimization runs synchronously and returns results fast enough for real-time use","Delivery failure is a terminal state for that delivery attempt (not retried on the same route)"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The optimization objective for route calculation is never specified. 'Optimized' could mean shortest distance, shortest time, highest priority first, maximum deliveries per hour, or a weighted combination. This fundamentally changes the algorithm, infrastructure requirements, and business outcomes. Different stakeholders likely have different expectations of what 'optimized' means.","affected_stakeholders":["Drivers","Operations Team","Engineering","Customers"],"options":["Optimize for shortest total travel time (most common in last-mile)","Optimize for shortest distance (fuel cost focus)","Optimize for delivery priority then time (SLA-driven)","Multi-objective optimization with configurable weights per zone"],"recommendation":"Default to shortest travel time as primary objective with delivery priority as a tiebreaker, since last-mile logistics almost universally optimizes for time. Make the objective configurable per zone by the operations team, consistent with the zone-level override pattern already established for stop time."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Manual deviation' has no defined detection mechanism. It could mean: the driver takes a different road (GPS path deviation), the driver skips a stop and goes to a later one (sequence deviation), or the driver drives away from all planned stops (abandonment). Each requires different detection logic and has different recalculation semantics.","affected_stakeholders":["Drivers","Engineering","Operations Team"],"options":["GPS geofence deviation — driver leaves corridor around planned path","Sequence deviation — driver arrives at a stop that is not the next expected stop","Explicit driver action — driver taps 'reroute' or 'skip' in the app","Combination: sequence deviation as primary, with GPS deviation as secondary"],"recommendation":"Define deviation as sequence deviation (driver completes or skips a stop out of order) as the primary trigger, since it is deterministic and does not require continuous GPS path matching. GPS path deviation is expensive to compute and prone to false positives from road detours."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The spec says the route includes 'all pending deliveries in their zone,' but does not address the scenario where multiple drivers are active in the same zone simultaneously. Assigning ALL pending deliveries to EACH driver creates duplicate assignments. The system needs a delivery partitioning or claiming strategy.","affected_stakeholders":["Drivers","Operations Team","Customers"],"options":["Partition deliveries geographically within the zone (sub-zones per driver)","Partition by optimization solver — assign to whichever driver yields the best overall fleet outcome","First-come assignment — each delivery assigned to the first driver whose route is calculated","Allow overlapping assignment with a claiming mechanism at pickup"],"recommendation":"Use optimization-based partitioning: when multiple drivers are in a zone, the route optimizer should consider all drivers simultaneously and partition deliveries to minimize total fleet travel time. This is the standard approach in vehicle routing problems (VRP) and avoids both duplication and suboptimal splits."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"No specification of what happens to customer notifications when recalculation moves a delivery out of the 'next 3 stops' window after a notification was already sent. The customer may have rearranged their schedule based on the notification.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Operations Team","Customer Support"],"options":["Send an updated notification with new ETA when delivery leaves the 3-stop window","Do not retract — accept that early notifications may become temporarily inaccurate","Send a 'delay' notification only if the delivery moves beyond a threshold (e.g., from stop 3 to stop 8+)"],"recommendation":"Send an updated notification if the delivery moves outside the 3-stop window after initial notification, with a simple message like 'Your delivery time has been updated.' Silence erodes trust more than an update does. Include threshold logic to avoid notification spam from minor reorderings."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}
